title: Pleural Effusion
oneLiner: Accumulation of fluid in pleural space; common in heart failure, infection, or malignancy—often a sign of underlying disease rather than primary pathology.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Tension Pneumothorax** — *Ipsilateral hyperresonance + tracheal deviation away + acute hypotension/hypoxia* before CXR. Immediate needle decompression.
- **Massive Pulmonary Embolism** — *Sudden dyspnoea + hypoxia + raised JVP with clear chest (no dullness)*. Do NOT attribute to effusion; get CT pulmonary angiogram.
- **Cardiac Tamponade** — *Muffled heart sounds + hypotension + raised JVP (Beck’s triad) + electrical alternans on ECG*. Perform bedside echo if available.
- **Empyema / Sepsis** — *Fever + hypotension + pleural fluid pH <7.2 or purulent fluid*. Requires urgent drainage and antibiotics.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Dyspnoea (exertional → at rest with size), pleuritic chest pain, cough (non-productive), orthopnoea (if large or bilateral)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced chest expansion on affected side
  - Stony dullness to percussion
  - Decreased breath sounds / bronchial breathing above effusion (compression)
  - Vocal resonance: *reduced* (effusion) vs *increased* (consolidation—helps differentiate)
  - Large effusion → mediastinal shift (trachea away from effusion)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Transudate (most common):** Heart failure (bilateral), cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, hypoalbuminaemia — *fluid protein <30 g/L, LDH <200 IU/L*
- **Exudate:** Parapneumonic (often unilateral, febrile), malignancy (often blood-stained), TB (younger, night sweats), PE (pleuritic pain + DVT), pancreatitis (left-sided), autoimmune (SLE, RA)
- **Splitter:** Check Light’s criteria (protein/LDH ratio) — *effusion protein/serum protein >0.5 OR effusion LDH/serum LDH >0.6 OR effusion LDH >2/3 upper normal serum LDH = exudate*. If exudate, consider infection/malignancy; if transudate, manage underlying cause.
- **Haemothorax:** Trauma, anticoagulation — *haematocrit >50% of peripheral blood*. Requires chest drain.
- **Chylothorax:** Milky fluid with triglycerides >1.24 mmol/L — thoracic duct injury.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - CXR (PA + lateral) – blunted costophrenic angle, fluid meniscus, loculation possible
  - Ultrasound (if available) – quantify size, guide aspiration, detect loculations
  - ECG – if suspect PE or tamponade
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, coagulation screen
  - NT-proBNP (if heart failure uncertain)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
  - TB risk: IGRA (Quantiferon) / T-SPOT
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CXR + bedside US
  - Second-line: CT thorax (if exudate, undiagnosed, or locate loculi)
- **Special tests:**
  - Diagnostic pleural aspiration – sent for: protein, LDH, glucose, pH, Gram stain, culture, cytology (cell count + malignant cells)
  - TB: AFB smear, culture, ADA (adenosine deaminase >35–40 U/L suggestive)
  - Lipase if pancreatitis suspected
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Light’s criteria (above) to differentiate transudate/exudate
- **Never-miss test:** **Pleural fluid pH** in suspected parapneumonic effusion – pH <7.2 indicates complicated effusion/empyema requiring drainage. Also **pleural fluid Gram stain & culture** before starting antibiotics (if safe to delay).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess for tension/tamponade/PE – if suspected, treat as per protocols (needle decompression, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis)
  - If large effusion causing respiratory distress (hypoxia, mediastinal shift) → **therapeutic thoracentesis** (ultrasound-guided if possible; drain up to 1–1.5 litres to prevent re-expansion pulmonary oedema)
  - If sign of infection (fever, toxic) → start **empiric antibiotics** (e.g., co-amoxiclav IV or piperacillin-tazobactam) after cultures, but do NOT delay antibiotics if septic
  - If empyema (purulent fluid, pH <7.2) → **urgent chest drain insertion** (size 12–14Fr)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Identify and treat cause:
    - Heart failure: diuresis (furosemide IV, monitor weight, U&E)
    - Pneumonia/parapneumonic: antibiotics as per local guidelines, drain if complicated
    - Malignancy: refer respiratory/oncology; consider pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheter
    - TB: start anti-TB therapy (seek ID/micro advice)
    - PE: anticoagulation (LMWH)
  - Monitor fluid output (if drain placed) – volume, colour, protein content
  - Daily CXR if drain in situ – confirm lung re-expansion, detect pneumothorax
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - F/u after 4–6 weeks CXR to confirm resolution
  - Recurrent effusion: consider pleurodesis (talc) or IPC
  - Manage underlying condition (HF, cirrhosis, malignancy)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: **IV maintenance** (e.g., 30 mL/kg/day of 0.9% NaCl or Hartmann’s) unless fluid overload (HF) → restrict to 1–1.5 L/day
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if no bleeding risk, e.g., haemothorax)
  - Diet: normal, unless NBM for procedures
  - Observations: **4-hourly** (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature); consider continuous SpO2 if large effusion
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Furosemide (HF-related transudate): 40 mg IV initially, then titrate; typical dose 40–80 mg PO/IV daily [check local policy for max dose]
  - Co-amoxiclav (parapneumonic, mild-moderate): 1.2 g IV TDS [check local microbiogram]
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam (severe/empyema): 4.5 g IV TDS [check local policy]
  - Pleurodesis (talc poudrage during thoracoscopy): 4–5 g insufflated
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ceftriaxone + metronidazole (if penicillin allergy): 2 g IV OD + 500 mg IV TDS
  - Doxycycline (for TB pleurisy as alternative)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Furosemide: avoid in anuria, caution with NSAIDs (reduced diuretic effect), monitor K+
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: caution in renal impairment (adjust dose)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Furosemide – no dose change in mild-moderate CKD; avoid in anuria. Piperacillin-tazobactam – adjust CrCl <20 mL/min: [check local policy for dosing]
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Furosemide – excessive dose in hypovolaemic patient** causing AKI + electrolyte disturbance. Always check baseline U&E, urine output, and assess volume status (JVP, oedema).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (if >1.5 L drained rapidly) → hypoxia, cough, frothy sputum. Prevent: drain slowly, stop if chest pain/discomfort, clamp drain if symptoms.
  - Empyema (if untreated complicated effusion)
  - Pneumothorax (post-aspiration or drain insertion)
  - Chest drain site infection, haemothorax, visceral injury
- **Chronic:**
  - Fibrothorax (thickened pleura, restrictive lung function) – often post-TB or empyema
  - Trapped lung (failure of lung to re-expand) – may require decortication
  - Recurrent malignant effusion (needs IPC or pleurodesis)
sections.Prognosis: - **Parapneumonic effusion:** excellent if drained early (empyema mortality <5% with treatment). **Malignant effusion:** poor, median survival 4–12 months depending on primary tumour. **HF-related:** resolves with diuresis. **TB pleurisy:** good with treatment, but 50% develop pleural thickening.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check pleural fluid pH** in any suspected parapneumonic effusion – pH <7.2 = drain.
- **Use ultrasound** for every pleural aspiration – reduces pneumothorax risk by 50%.
- **If draining large effusion (>1 L), consider pleural manometry** or clamp drain after 1 L to check for chest pain (sign of high negative pressure).
- **Send fluid for TB culture** even if AFB smear negative – ADA >35 U/L is supportive.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT drain >1.5 L in one session** without monitoring – risk of re-expansion pulmonary oedema.
- **Do NOT start antibiotics before taking pleural fluid cultures** if patient stable – you need microbiology to guide targeted therapy.
- **Do NOT ignore loculated effusion** – requires CT or US-guided drainage; simple needle may miss.
- **Do NOT diagnose “pleural effusion” and stop – always determine whether transudate or exudate** using Light’s criteria.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Worsening dyspnoea, fever/chills, new chest pain, or cough productive of blood
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic in 4–6 weeks with repeat CXR; if malignant or recurrent, refer to respiratory physician for IPC/pleurodesis
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/reg if:**
  - NEWS2 >5 or rising
  - Persistent hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on FiO2 >0.4)
  - Hypotension (sBP <90 mmHg) not responding to fluids
  - Signs of tension (tracheal deviation, shock) – immediate needle decompression
  - GCS <14 or seizure
- **Call ICU/CRASH team if:**
  - Cardiac arrest, respiratory arrest, or profound shock (lactate >4 mmol/L)
  - Anaphylaxis (to antibiotic/local anaesthetic)
  - Massive haemothorax (>1500 mL on drain insertion)

rawMarkdown:
## Pleural Effusion
**One-liner:** Accumulation of fluid in pleural space; common in heart failure, infection, or malignancy—often a sign of underlying disease rather than primary pathology.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Tension Pneumothorax** — *Ipsilateral hyperresonance + tracheal deviation away + acute hypotension/hypoxia* before CXR. Immediate needle decompression.
- **Massive Pulmonary Embolism** — *Sudden dyspnoea + hypoxia + raised JVP with clear chest (no dullness)*. Do NOT attribute to effusion; get CT pulmonary angiogram.
- **Cardiac Tamponade** — *Muffled heart sounds + hypotension + raised JVP (Beck’s triad) + electrical alternans on ECG*. Perform bedside echo if available.
- **Empyema / Sepsis** — *Fever + hypotension + pleural fluid pH <7.2 or purulent fluid*. Requires urgent drainage and antibiotics.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** Dyspnoea (exertional → at rest with size), pleuritic chest pain, cough (non-productive), orthopnoea (if large or bilateral)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Reduced chest expansion on affected side
  - Stony dullness to percussion
  - Decreased breath sounds / bronchial breathing above effusion (compression)
  - Vocal resonance: *reduced* (effusion) vs *increased* (consolidation—helps differentiate)
  - Large effusion → mediastinal shift (trachea away from effusion)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Transudate (most common):** Heart failure (bilateral), cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, hypoalbuminaemia — *fluid protein <30 g/L, LDH <200 IU/L*
- **Exudate:** Parapneumonic (often unilateral, febrile), malignancy (often blood-stained), TB (younger, night sweats), PE (pleuritic pain + DVT), pancreatitis (left-sided), autoimmune (SLE, RA)
- **Splitter:** Check Light’s criteria (protein/LDH ratio) — *effusion protein/serum protein >0.5 OR effusion LDH/serum LDH >0.6 OR effusion LDH >2/3 upper normal serum LDH = exudate*. If exudate, consider infection/malignancy; if transudate, manage underlying cause.
- **Haemothorax:** Trauma, anticoagulation — *haematocrit >50% of peripheral blood*. Requires chest drain.
- **Chylothorax:** Milky fluid with triglycerides >1.24 mmol/L — thoracic duct injury.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - CXR (PA + lateral) – blunted costophrenic angle, fluid meniscus, loculation possible
  - Ultrasound (if available) – quantify size, guide aspiration, detect loculations
  - ECG – if suspect PE or tamponade
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, coagulation screen
  - NT-proBNP (if heart failure uncertain)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
  - TB risk: IGRA (Quantiferon) / T-SPOT
- **Imaging:**
  - First-line: CXR + bedside US
  - Second-line: CT thorax (if exudate, undiagnosed, or locate loculi)
- **Special tests:**
  - Diagnostic pleural aspiration – sent for: protein, LDH, glucose, pH, Gram stain, culture, cytology (cell count + malignant cells)
  - TB: AFB smear, culture, ADA (adenosine deaminase >35–40 U/L suggestive)
  - Lipase if pancreatitis suspected
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Light’s criteria (above) to differentiate transudate/exudate
- **Never-miss test:** **Pleural fluid pH** in suspected parapneumonic effusion – pH <7.2 indicates complicated effusion/empyema requiring drainage. Also **pleural fluid Gram stain & culture** before starting antibiotics (if safe to delay).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Assess for tension/tamponade/PE – if suspected, treat as per protocols (needle decompression, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis)
  - If large effusion causing respiratory distress (hypoxia, mediastinal shift) → **therapeutic thoracentesis** (ultrasound-guided if possible; drain up to 1–1.5 litres to prevent re-expansion pulmonary oedema)
  - If sign of infection (fever, toxic) → start **empiric antibiotics** (e.g., co-amoxiclav IV or piperacillin-tazobactam) after cultures, but do NOT delay antibiotics if septic
  - If empyema (purulent fluid, pH <7.2) → **urgent chest drain insertion** (size 12–14Fr)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - Identify and treat cause:
    - Heart failure: diuresis (furosemide IV, monitor weight, U&E)
    - Pneumonia/parapneumonic: antibiotics as per local guidelines, drain if complicated
    - Malignancy: refer respiratory/oncology; consider pleurodesis or indwelling pleural catheter
    - TB: start anti-TB therapy (seek ID/micro advice)
    - PE: anticoagulation (LMWH)
  - Monitor fluid output (if drain placed) – volume, colour, protein content
  - Daily CXR if drain in situ – confirm lung re-expansion, detect pneumothorax
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - F/u after 4–6 weeks CXR to confirm resolution
  - Recurrent effusion: consider pleurodesis (talc) or IPC
  - Manage underlying condition (HF, cirrhosis, malignancy)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: **IV maintenance** (e.g., 30 mL/kg/day of 0.9% NaCl or Hartmann’s) unless fluid overload (HF) → restrict to 1–1.5 L/day
  - VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if no bleeding risk, e.g., haemothorax)
  - Diet: normal, unless NBM for procedures
  - Observations: **4-hourly** (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature); consider continuous SpO2 if large effusion

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Furosemide (HF-related transudate): 40 mg IV initially, then titrate; typical dose 40–80 mg PO/IV daily [check local policy for max dose]
  - Co-amoxiclav (parapneumonic, mild-moderate): 1.2 g IV TDS [check local microbiogram]
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam (severe/empyema): 4.5 g IV TDS [check local policy]
  - Pleurodesis (talc poudrage during thoracoscopy): 4–5 g insufflated
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Ceftriaxone + metronidazole (if penicillin allergy): 2 g IV OD + 500 mg IV TDS
  - Doxycycline (for TB pleurisy as alternative)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Furosemide: avoid in anuria, caution with NSAIDs (reduced diuretic effect), monitor K+
  - Piperacillin-tazobactam: caution in renal impairment (adjust dose)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Furosemide – no dose change in mild-moderate CKD; avoid in anuria. Piperacillin-tazobactam – adjust CrCl <20 mL/min: [check local policy for dosing]
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Furosemide – excessive dose in hypovolaemic patient** causing AKI + electrolyte disturbance. Always check baseline U&E, urine output, and assess volume status (JVP, oedema).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Re-expansion pulmonary oedema (if >1.5 L drained rapidly) → hypoxia, cough, frothy sputum. Prevent: drain slowly, stop if chest pain/discomfort, clamp drain if symptoms.
  - Empyema (if untreated complicated effusion)
  - Pneumothorax (post-aspiration or drain insertion)
  - Chest drain site infection, haemothorax, visceral injury
- **Chronic:**
  - Fibrothorax (thickened pleura, restrictive lung function) – often post-TB or empyema
  - Trapped lung (failure of lung to re-expand) – may require decortication
  - Recurrent malignant effusion (needs IPC or pleurodesis)

### Prognosis
- **Parapneumonic effusion:** excellent if drained early (empyema mortality <5% with treatment). **Malignant effusion:** poor, median survival 4–12 months depending on primary tumour. **HF-related:** resolves with diuresis. **TB pleurisy:** good with treatment, but 50% develop pleural thickening.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check pleural fluid pH** in any suspected parapneumonic effusion – pH <7.2 = drain.
- **Use ultrasound** for every pleural aspiration – reduces pneumothorax risk by 50%.
- **If draining large effusion (>1 L), consider pleural manometry** or clamp drain after 1 L to check for chest pain (sign of high negative pressure).
- **Send fluid for TB culture** even if AFB smear negative – ADA >35 U/L is supportive.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT drain >1.5 L in one session** without monitoring – risk of re-expansion pulmonary oedema.
- **Do NOT start antibiotics before taking pleural fluid cultures** if patient stable – you need microbiology to guide targeted therapy.
- **Do NOT ignore loculated effusion** – requires CT or US-guided drainage; simple needle may miss.
- **Do NOT diagnose “pleural effusion” and stop – always determine whether transudate or exudate** using Light’s criteria.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Worsening dyspnoea, fever/chills, new chest pain, or cough productive of blood
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic in 4–6 weeks with repeat CXR; if malignant or recurrent, refer to respiratory physician for IPC/pleurodesis

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/reg if:**
  - NEWS2 >5 or rising
  - Persistent hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on FiO2 >0.4)
  - Hypotension (sBP <90 mmHg) not responding to fluids
  - Signs of tension (tracheal deviation, shock) – immediate needle decompression
  - GCS <14 or seizure
- **Call ICU/CRASH team if:**
  - Cardiac arrest, respiratory arrest, or profound shock (lactate >4 mmol/L)
  - Anaphylaxis (to antibiotic/local anaesthetic)
  - Massive haemothorax (>1500 mL on drain insertion)
